# Preface {.unnumbered}

Every team that manages structured data eventually hits the same wall. You change a schema---add a field, rename a column, tighten a constraint---and something downstream breaks. A Protobuf client crashes on an unknown field number. A Kafka consumer rejects a message that no longer matches the registry. A SQL migration takes the database offline because it can't rewrite a table with foreign key dependents.

The tools exist to handle each of these individually. [Buf](https://buf.build/) catches breaking Protobuf changes. [Atlas](https://atlasgo.io/) manages SQL DDL. [Confluent Schema Registry](https://docs.confluent.io/platform/current/schema-registry/) enforces Avro compatibility. [graphql-inspector](https://the-guild.dev/graphql/inspector) flags GraphQL regressions. But the underlying problem is identical every time: you have a source schema, a target schema, and data that needs to move from one to the other. The only thing that varies is what "schema" and "data" mean.

**panproto** is a schematic version control system. Instead of hardcoding a particular schema language, it parameterizes over the *theory* (a formal description of a schema language's structural vocabulary — what kinds of entities, relationships, and constraints it supports) that defines what schemas look like and a separate theory describing what data looks like in that language. Migration validation, data transformation, bidirectional lenses, breaking-change detection---all derived from those two theories by the same algorithms, regardless of whether you are working with ATProto Lexicons, SQL DDL, Protobuf, GraphQL, or any of the [76 protocols panproto supports](appendices/C-protocol-catalog.qmd).

## What this tutorial covers

The tutorial is organized in six parts:

- **Part I** opens with the problem: why schema migration is hard, and why existing tools are protocol-specific.
- **Part II** introduces the structural foundation: what all schemas have in common, and how panproto parameterizes over their differences.
- **Part III** walks through migration end-to-end: writing, validating, and executing migrations, with bidirectional lenses and CI integration.
- **Part IV** covers the practical toolkit: data lifting, custom protocols, schema version control, and cross-protocol translation.
- **Part V** goes deeper: naming, automatic migration discovery, and the building-block theory library.
- **Part VI** introduces automated lenses: protolenses (reusable lens families), automatic lens generation, and symmetric lenses for cross-schema sync.

## Who this is for

You write software that handles structured data. You know what a Protobuf message, a SQL table, or a JSON Schema looks like. No category theory background is required---the tutorial introduces every concept it uses in practical terms, with formal foundations available in [Appendix A](appendices/A-formal-foundations.qmd) for those who want the mathematical details.

## Installation

::: {.panel-tabset}

### TypeScript

```bash
npm install @panproto/core
```

### Rust

```toml
[dependencies]
panproto-core = "0.6"
```

### Python

```bash
pip install panproto
```

:::

Code examples use the panproto TypeScript SDK (`@panproto/core`), Rust API (`panproto-core`), and Python SDK (`panproto`). All code is pulled from source files using the [`include-code-files`](https://github.com/quarto-ext/include-code-files) Quarto extension, so what you see matches the implementation.
